desc: Faro is the Algarve’s understated capital, mixing a walled old town with wetlands, island beaches, and excellent seafood. It works well as both a relaxed city break and a practical base for exploring the Ria Formosa lagoon and nearby coastal towns.
hls: Historic Cidade Velha with Arco da Vila gate
Ria Formosa lagoon ecosystem and birdlife
Boat access to Ilha Deserta and Ilha Farol
Strong Algarve seafood and cataplana options
More local pace than western Algarve resorts

todo.name: Explore Faro old town walls
todo.desc: Walk through Arco da Vila to see cathedral squares, cobbled lanes, and city-wall viewpoints.

todo.name: Boat trip in Ria Formosa
todo.desc: Join a lagoon tour for sandbar islands, birdwatching, and channels that shift with the tides.

todo.name: Beach day on Ilha Deserta or Farol
todo.desc: Take the ferry and spend a full day swimming and walking long Atlantic sand stretches.

todo.name: Municipal Museum and cathedral tower
todo.desc: Pair Roman and medieval exhibits with tower views over the old town and marshlands.

todo.name: Seafood dinner in Faro marina area
todo.desc: Try grilled fish, octopus rice, and Algarve-style shellfish in waterfront restaurants.

todo.name: Day trip to Tavira or Olhão
todo.desc: Use short train rides to visit tiled river towns and traditional fishing-port markets.

wifi.note: Strong in central Faro and resort areas; some rural Algarve properties still have slower DSL.

prac.fltNote: Gatwick–Faro has frequent nonstop flights, especially from spring through autumn.
prac.visa: Portugal is in Schengen; many travelers (including US/UK) can stay up to 90 days in any 180-day period visa-free.
alerts: Rental cars and beach accommodation sell out quickly in July and August.

sweet: April to June and September to October bring sunny beach weather without the busiest midsummer crowds.
